questions.question: Why is durability better described as a tiered promise than a single yes-or-no property?
options: Because different data classes justify different replication, coding, and repair-time guarantees.
Because durable systems never need repair jobs.
Because storage vendors are required to expose at least three pricing plans.
Because one replica in memory is enough for all archival data.
questions.explanation: Durability depends on how valuable the data is and what failures you are protecting against.

questions.question: What makes a 'three copies' durability claim misleading by itself?
options: Replica count is meaningless unless placement and repair assumptions are also clear.
Three is an unlucky number in distributed systems.
It always means the system uses synchronous cross-region writes.
It prevents erasure coding in all cases.
questions.explanation: Copies in the wrong places or repaired too slowly can still leave weak durability.

questions.question: Why might a hot tier choose full replicas instead of erasure coding?
options: To keep reads simple and repairs faster for latency-sensitive serving paths.
Because erasure coding is impossible above one terabyte.
Because replicated data never needs audits.
To avoid documenting repair objectives.
questions.explanation: Latency and repair simplicity can matter more than raw storage efficiency on hot paths.

questions.question: Which metric best shows whether durability promises are being restored quickly enough?
options: Objects below target durability by tier and age.
Average markdown file size.
Only total object count.
The number of pull requests merged this week.
questions.explanation: Aging degraded objects directly measure vulnerability window.

questions.question: What trade-off comes with offering many durability tiers to customers?
options: Better fit for business needs, but more policy, support, and migration complexity.
Guaranteed lower cost with no operational impact.
No need for safe defaults anymore.
Repair logic becomes unnecessary.
questions.explanation: Flexibility helps, but it raises explanation and policy-management costs.

questions.question: Why can slow repair be a bigger durability risk than an occasionally failed disk?
options: Because it leaves the system exposed for longer, increasing the chance that another failure causes actual loss.
Because disks never fail twice.
Because repair speed only affects cost and not safety.
Because replication disables all correlated failure modes.
questions.explanation: Durability is partly about how long you remain degraded after the first failure.

questions.question: What is the best reason to test a geo-redundant tier with real restore drills?
options: To verify that independence and recovery work in practice rather than only in placement diagrams.
To decrease object checksum size.
To remove the need for replication policies.
To make every tier equally cheap.
questions.explanation: Regional restore is an operational property that must be proven.

questions.question: Which follow-up most changes the durability design?
options: Customers can now move objects between standard and compliance tiers without downtime.
The team wants different font choices in internal docs.
The bucket names should be shorter.
One chart should use thicker lines.
questions.explanation: Tier migration introduces policy, state, and repair workflow changes.
